speaker: interviewer
text: Reel three. OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not that important to have a time code, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I have time to take a little water?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's OK. Go ahead. I'm going to start the tape. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is tape three, interviewing Laura Hillman in Los Alamitos. March 17, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Hillman, tell me more about working in the kitchen with the SS while you were at Brunnlitz. And tell me more about Oskar Schindler and your contact with him. I didn't have that much contact with Oskar Schindler. I saw him stride into the camp. And, as I said before, he came to the kitchen occasionally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know him that well, really, other than that I knew that through him we were getting more food. My husband was in the underground. Bernard Hillman was in the underground. I knew from him that because of Schindler and his connection with the partisans, that we had gotten a lot of extra food. We were not hungry in Brunnlitz, none of us. It wasn't excess, but we were not hungry. And the SS men allowed me to eat the leftovers from the day before, but only when he said so. They were kept in one corner. I would never go and take it myself. Only wait till he told me. Could never take anything out of the kitchen. I was careful not to do it. And I had to keep myself clean, of course, and that wasn't the easiest thing to do because washing in-- I think we got a shower once a week in Brunnlitz. That was the greatest luxury ever. And so, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I made myself as invisible as I could. I worked very hard, constantly. I never stood idle. And when there was nothing to do, I took a rag and I wiped again and again things that I'd already done three times that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left you pretty much alone? Nobody tried anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the man in charge would talk to me, give barking orders at me, but never engaged me in any conversation until the very end. When it was obvious that the Russians were coming closer and he'd say, but you're going to tell them how well we treated you. And I said, [GERMAN] meaning, of course, of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what happened towards the end of your stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had heard from Bernard Hillman already, who had a radio, that things were coming to an end. The Russians were coming closer, but that we weren't safe yet because Leipold, Josef Leipold, wanted to have us all shot. I believe they were digging already graves around the camp. And our only hope was that Schindler was going to get him transferred. And Oskar Schindler was away a lot at that time, and there were rumors that he was arrested, you know. I never knew the real truth. But we felt insecure without Oskar Schindler. And one day he did return to the very end, and not long after that Leipold was sent away. And without Leipold there, we knew there was a chance for us. Oskar Schindler called us one day into the factory. I had to come from the kitchen too. And he told us that he had listened to Churchill's speech, and it was all over. And he cautioned us not to take the law into our own hands. There were others. I happen to know that one couple was hanged. I learned about that later. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were left alone for two days. Bernard Hillman stood at the camp entrance with two-- what do you call that? Rifles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifles. And some of the other prisoners of war and the others guarding the camp because the Germans could come back in and do us harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans had already-- your SS and all of them had already left at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had left, but there were stragglers coming through, you know, from other places too. And we really were not secured yet. There was a lot of shooting outside. And there was the possibility that the Germans could come back one more time and just destroy what they could and shoot whom they could shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an uneasiness until May 10th. I remember that day forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very small man, a Russian, came in on a horse, rolled in on a horse, and he dismounted. And my husband, later my husband, Bernard Hillman, said to me, 'I'll tell you what he said,' because he spoke Russian. I did not. He said, 'He's a Hebrew, a Jew.' And, in essence, what he said was, like you, I'm a Hebrew, I'm a Jew. And, therefore, I should free you. That's symbolic, that's right. I should free you. And, of course, we laughed and we cried. And you could hear every language, Slavic language, being, 'We're free! We're free! We made it! We made it!' And there was a lot of crying too. And I went back to our barrack and I thought I was dreaming. I was in the ovens and screaming, 'Get me out! Get me out!' And nobody would come, and this sort of thing. That reality of it came only very slowly. Although we knew we were free, but couldn't really believe it had happened. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there before you left to go somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed a few days only. And we got material and vodka. I got a navy blue serge, and I have wedding picture. My wedding dress was made out of that serge because that same year, on October 22, 1945, Bernard Hillman and I made it back to Germany looking for relatives and got married. And the army, the United States Army, a Jewish chaplain, and Jewish officers made the wedding and helped us through that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see Schindler leave from Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not see him leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after Brunnlitz, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, in Brunnlitz one day, a day after the Russian came in, a couple came in with 10-year-old twins. They were from Nitra, a town in Czechoslovakia. And they related their story. They had post as Quakers and thereby survived the war. And now they were so grateful, they'd like to take some young girls with them to Nitra to help them build a new life. And they selected my friend, Ava, and myself. Bernard Hillman said to me, I love you, but I have to go back to Poland. I have to see if my family is there. His brother [INAUDIBLE] had gone with the Russians. He thought maybe he'd come back. His mother, his sisters. And he had left a girl behind and he had to tell her that things had changed. And I told him where I was going. And I went with the Messengers was their name. His brother was the dean of Columbia University, at the time. And the Messengers took Ava and myself, and we lived in their house, and we did housework and everything. And they tried to get husbands for us. But we never felt good in that house. The 10-year-old twins called us [NON-ENGLISH], Germans, because to them we were Germans. We spoke German. We didn't speak Slavic languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, one Sunday afternoon, the bell rang and we were all sleeping. We had just come from a football game, or soccer game. I heard the bell, and I went to the bell, and who was standing at the door? Bernard Hillman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I fell into this arms and I said, 'Take me away. They've selected a husband for me I don't want. He's 30 years older than I am. He's a tailor. And they say he's a good catch, but I love you.' Well, anyway, to make a long story short, he took Ava and me and we traveled to Paris and to Prague. Bernard Hillman had a cousin who had gone to medical school there. He was looking for her. Her name was [INAUDIBLE]. We went everywhere. To police stations, everywhere. We couldn't find her. We were in a Swiss Hotel at the time, Ava, Bernard, and I, and some other survivors. And we found that we could not get out of that area. We had to have a three-zone pass-- English, Russian, and American. It was unobtainable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Bernard spoke Slavic languages very well. He also had our vodka. And after weeks he bribed someone and we got our three-zone pass. We made our way to Landsberg am Lech, where we were in a-- what was it? DP camp. Again, I was very ill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to my hometown from there. I wanted to go to the cemetery. No, I didn't go there, excuse me. I went later. Strike that. I went later. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed in Erding. We stayed in Landsberg for a little while, and moved to Erding, where we got married. And my husband had an uncle in the United States. And I had met a childhood friend who was in the brigade from Israel. And he said, I can dress you up as a boy. I know where your sister lives. Your sister is married. I didn't know any of that. And I believe she has a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my sister, Hildegard, down to Israel. And I was in love I didn't want to go to Israel with the brigade. And in Landsberg am Lech, I went to the UNRRA and I spoke to an English woman who was an officer there. And I said, I have a sister who lived in Manchester, England. She immigrated. I believe she became a nurse. Her name is Rosel Wolff.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that woman shrieked. She said, she's also the Hebrew teacher in this and this synagogue. And is-- are your relatives [INAUDIBLE], who have the hotel in Manchester?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew Rosel. She had seen her two weeks before. She immediately got in touch with her, and also gave me food, and vitamin C, and everything. Of course, that was the first connection with that sister. And then this young man that I met wasn't going back to Israel, so the Red Cross, I think, sent a letter to her. Unfortunately, I spelled everything out. I shouldn't have done that in a first letter. And you saw the response to the letter I showed to you, the first letter that came from her. Well, Bernard Hillman and I, after we got married, we tried very hard to get to the United States. That's where I wanted to be. And, finally, we got our visas, and we were stuck in Bremen, in Bremerhaven. There was a ship strike.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there I went to my hometown. It was nearby. And I came to one of the houses where we had lived. And the woman opened the door and I saw our furniture there. And she said, who are you? You look like Hannelore. And I said. Yes. I am her. And she shut the door on me. You're not supposed to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran and ran away. And I went to the cemetery to visit the graves of my grandparents and people I knew, and I ran from the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't try to get any of your property back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw when I saw the movie, when I saw the movie, I suddenly realized that my mother ended the way it's shown in the movie. I never heard from her again. I believe I know when she died to [INAUDIBLE]. I keep yahrzeit for her and for my father. As I said before, at that time they send an urn yet. In March of 1942, in the beginning, they send an urn and a letter, so-and-so died. So I know my father's yahrzeit. I keep a yahrzeit, of sorts, for my brothers. Whether it's correct or not, I don't know, but it gives me comfort to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you finally get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on January 4, 1947, we were on the the and when we got off in New York there was this big, burly man who resembled my husband very much. And they kissed and hugged. And Benu, Benu, how are you? And who is this? Did you bring a child with you? And he said, that's my wife, Hannelore. And he said, No, no Hannelores here. Laura. Let's name her Laura. And I later changed it legally. He was very good to us. And it was a hard struggle. I was very ill for many years with a rare blood disease. And I didn't have children for nine years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then my son was born in 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. And I had to be under the supervision of several doctors and stay in bed for the entire time. And it was wonderful, wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is he named after?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's named after my husband's mother and sister. Their name was Regina, and so we made a Robert out of it. And Steven after my brother Selly, his middle name. [SOBBING] Oh, God. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] He grew up to be exceptional. Exceptional. I have such a warm relationship with him. I always have. And when he went away to school and he saw which was really a white wash, we changed roles. He was only a young man in college, but he became the parent and we became his children. And that's how it's been ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do for a living in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband owned a liquor store in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1957. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I taught in a nursery school. I went back to school and taught in a cooperative nursery school that was associated with Long Beach State, with the Home Economics Department. And then, after my husband died, I went back to school one more time for three and a half years to Long Beach State.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- he died in 1986. In 1987 to 1990, I learned how to write, I hope. I wrote my book there. I took upper division [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] English classes and novel writing and construction, and so on. Found a wonderful professor, who was very helpful and took me on on independent study later. Was very helpful. And I think that was catharsis for me and it was very good that I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Hillman, let me ask you, now that it's 50 years after World War II, is there any message that you'd like to give your son, your grandchildren, the world, about your experiences and what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No matter how deep you sink in life and how many awful experiences you will have, it's up to you to raise yourself up and start a life that can be productive, and useful, and helpful. You don't have to stay down all the time. You can make it. And only you, yourself, can do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I look back at Auschwitz-- and I was recently at the Taj Mahal where I met the Israeli ambassador to India. And he said to me, how do you like it? And I said to him, 'It's a long road from Auschwitz to the Taj Mahal.' And he said to me, you summed it up very well. So it's up to us. This happened to me. I will never, never forgive the Germans. Never. They robbed me off my childhood, of my parents, of everything that could have been loving and kind to me. I cannot forgive them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that doesn't mean that I live with hatred. I work at the museum with German women and, you know, I don't hate the individual. But the Nazis, the perpetrators, I can't forgive them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I don't want my children, my son, to forget, or his children to forget, where I came from and what I went through. And then I want them to realize what I was-- a happy, outgoing woman who laughed with them and played with them. I want them to remember me how they knew me. But, yet, they should know where I came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have some pictures you'd like to share and with us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're looking at my paternal grandparents, Wolf Wolff and Rosette Wolff, [GERMAN] [INAUDIBLE]. Rosette was killed somewhere in Poland, I don't know where. Wolf [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] died early on. I remember him just vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turn of the century?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was probably taken around the turn of the century, I would imagine. Yeah. 1910 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Aurich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, Selli Wolff and Henriette Wolff, [GERMAN] [INAUDIBLE]. Henriette died in Theresienstadt. Opa Selli died in 1933, I believe. This was taken in one of the spas, maybe Bad Nauheim. I'm not certain. It would be around 1920 or so. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, Martin and Karoline Wolff, [GERMAN] Wolff. This was at the time of their engagement. It was probably 1918 or 1919 in Aurich, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 37 SECONDS] These are my two brothers, Wolfgang and Selly Wolff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolfgang is the shorter one, but he's also the older one. Selly was the youngest. He's husky and big. They were about 11 and 12 and a half at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Cologne, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been 1940. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, now I can really see them. My God, what a difference. And I could have this done, huh? I must do it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Used to say, are you Rosel Wolff's sister? She was so beautiful. She lives in Dublin, Ireland today. Go ahead, start.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my sisters, Rosel, Rosel Wolff Siev, who now lives in Ireland. Dublin, Ireland. This was taken in 1939, March of 1939, in Aurich, one day before she left Germany to go to England. This is my sister, Hildgard who went to Yugoslavia in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Boarded the the ship that landed in Haifa, to then Palestine. Hilde died in 1978 of leukemia.

speaker: interviewer
text: These pictures were taken where? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where were these pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is my father, Martin Wolff. The last picture of him. Taken in about January of 1942, as they did forced labor, filling sacks with potatoes and then standing there waiting to sell them. Shortly after that he was taken to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Weimar, Thuringia. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is Dr. Frankel's kindergarten seminar, private Jewish girl's school, in Berlin, in Grunewald.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where I went to school, where I got the news of my father's death and the upcoming deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1942, prior to my leaving the school. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This document came to me recently from a woman in Weimar. It's a duplicate of the paper that was issued and showed that my father was arrested and brought to Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 44 SECONDS] This is a copy of Schindler's List. I'm number 287, Hannelore Wolff. [PAUSES FOR 65 SECONDS] This is, again, Schindler's List. Prisoner number 390 was my late husband, Bernard Hillman. [PAUSES FOR 31 SECONDS] '37 before he was conscribed into the Polish army. This is a picture of Hannelore Wolff, now Laura Hillman, taken in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Weimar, Thuring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you husband's picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in Poland. I don't know where. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Bernard and Laura Hillman with a Jewish army chaplain in Erding on October 22, 1945. My wedding dress is made out of material Oskar Schindler gave me. It was Navy blue serge, intended for German uniforms, Navy uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] In Manchester, England, in 1950, the first reunion with my sisters, Rosel and Hildegard. Hilde came from Israel and I came from the United States. The first time we'd seen each other since 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's which?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the right is Rosel. The one on the left was Hildegard. And the middle one is Laura Hillman.
